title: What is the Boyer-Moore string matching algorithm?
description: How does Boyer-Moore work?
options: Uses bad character and good suffix heuristics to skip comparisons; compares pattern right-to-left; achieves sub-linear average time O(n/m) for text search, best practical string matching algorithm
correct_answer: Uses bad character and good suffix heuristics to skip comparisons; compares pattern right-to-left; achieves sub-linear average time O(n/m) for text search, best practical string matching algorithm

title: What is the difference between BFS and DFS for shortest path?
description: When should you use BFS over DFS for pathfinding?
options: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs (explores level by level); DFS may find longer paths first. BFS uses queue O(V+E); DFS uses stack/recursion O(V+E) but uses less memory for deep graphs
correct_answer: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs (explores level by level); DFS may find longer paths first. BFS uses queue O(V+E); DFS uses stack/recursion O(V+E) but uses less memory for deep graphs

title: What is the time complexity of common sorting algorithms?
description: Compare time complexities of sorting algorithms.
options: Bubble/Selection/Insertion: O(n²) avg; Merge Sort: O(n log n) always; Quick Sort: O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst; Heap Sort: O(n log n) always; Counting/Radix: O(nk) linear for bounded integers
correct_answer: Bubble/Selection/Insertion: O(n²) avg; Merge Sort: O(n log n) always; Quick Sort: O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst; Heap Sort: O(n log n) always; Counting/Radix: O(nk) linear for bounded integers

title: What is amortized time complexity?
description: Explain amortized analysis.
options: Averages the cost of operations over a sequence; e.g., dynamic array append is O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resizing. Methods: aggregate, accounting, potential. Different from average-case analysis
correct_answer: Averages the cost of operations over a sequence; e.g., dynamic array append is O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resizing. Methods: aggregate, accounting, potential. Different from average-case analysis

title: What is the difference between greedy and dynamic programming?
description: When should you use greedy vs DP?
options: Greedy makes locally optimal choices hoping for global optimum (requires greedy choice property and optimal substructure); DP solves all subproblems and combines results (handles overlapping subproblems). DP always gives correct answer; greedy may not
correct_answer: Greedy makes locally optimal choices hoping for global optimum (requires greedy choice property and optimal substructure); DP solves all subproblems and combines results (handles overlapping subproblems). DP always gives correct answer; greedy may not

title: Implement a graph topological sort using Kahn's algorithm.
description: Write topological sort using BFS (Kahn's).
correct_answer:
from collections import deque

def topological_sort(num_nodes, edges):
    in_degree = [0] * num_nodes
    adj = [[] for _ in range(num_nodes)]
    for u, v in edges:
        adj[u].append(v)
        in_degree[v] += 1

    queue = deque([i for i in range(num_nodes) if in_degree[i] == 0])
    result = []

    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        result.append(node)
        for neighbor in adj[node]:
            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
                queue.append(neighbor)

    return result if len(result) == num_nodes else []  # empty if cycle exists

title: What is the sliding window maximum problem?
description: How do you solve sliding window maximum efficiently?
options: Use a monotonic decreasing deque; as window slides, remove elements outside window and smaller elements from back; front always holds the maximum. O(n) time, O(k) space where k is window size
Sort each window O(nk log k)
Use a heap O(n log n)
correct_answer: Use a monotonic decreasing deque; as window slides, remove elements outside window and smaller elements from back; front always holds the maximum. O(n) time, O(k) space where k is window size

title: What is the difference between adjacency list and adjacency matrix?
description: Compare graph representations.
options: Adjacency list: array of lists, O(V+E) space, O(degree) edge lookup, better for sparse graphs. Adjacency matrix: V×V 2D array, O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup, better for dense graphs
correct_answer: Adjacency list: array of lists, O(V+E) space, O(degree) edge lookup, better for sparse graphs. Adjacency matrix: V×V 2D array, O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup, better for dense graphs

title: What is tail recursion?
description: What is tail recursion and why does it matter?
options: A recursive call that is the last operation in the function; can be optimized by compilers into iteration (tail call optimization) using constant stack space. Python doesn't optimize it, but it's important conceptually
correct_answer: A recursive call that is the last operation in the function; can be optimized by compilers into iteration (tail call optimization) using constant stack space. Python doesn't optimize it, but it's important conceptually

title: What is the difference between stable and unstable sorting?
description: Why does sort stability matter?
options: Stable sort preserves the relative order of equal elements (merge sort, insertion sort, timsort); unstable may change it (quicksort, heapsort). Matters when sorting by multiple criteria sequentially
correct_answer: Stable sort preserves the relative order of equal elements (merge sort, insertion sort, timsort); unstable may change it (quicksort, heapsort). Matters when sorting by multiple criteria sequentially

title: What is memoization vs tabulation?
description: Compare top-down and bottom-up DP.
options: Memoization (top-down): recursive with cache, solves only needed subproblems, easier to implement. Tabulation (bottom-up): iterative, fills table systematically, no recursion overhead, can optimize space. Both achieve same time complexity
correct_answer: Memoization (top-down): recursive with cache, solves only needed subproblems, easier to implement. Tabulation (bottom-up): iterative, fills table systematically, no recursion overhead, can optimize space. Both achieve same time complexity

title: What is the difference between NP, NP-hard, and NP-complete?
description: Explain computational complexity classes.
options: NP: problems verifiable in polynomial time. NP-hard: at least as hard as NP problems (may not be in NP). NP-complete: both in NP and NP-hard (e.g., SAT, TSP decision version, subset sum). P = NP is unsolved
correct_answer: NP: problems verifiable in polynomial time. NP-hard: at least as hard as NP problems (may not be in NP). NP-complete: both in NP and NP-hard (e.g., SAT, TSP decision version, subset sum). P = NP is unsolved
